title: F16 Characterization Theorem
informal_summary: A function f: R -> R is exactly representable as a finite F16 tree if and only if f belongs to the exp-log closure ELC(R): the class of functions expressible as finite algebraic expressions in the primitives {exp, ln} over rational constants, closed under the four arithmetic operations {+, -, *, /} and composition.

F16_operators.description: The 16 binary operators O(x,y) formed by pairing each of {exp(x), exp(-x), ln(x)} as outer function with each of {x+y, x-y, x*y, x/y} as argument, plus the 4 arithmetic operators themselves

named_operators.LEdiv: ln(x / y)  [x/y>0], equivalently x - ln(y) when x = ln(something)
named_operators.note: exact enumeration: 16 operators is the full family; source: d1_operator_zoo.json

F16_tree.description: A finite binary tree whose internal nodes are F16 operators, leaves are real variables or rational constants, and the tree computes a function via composition in the natural way
F16_tree.node_count: number of internal (operator) nodes; leaves are free
F16_tree.domain: the maximal subset of R^n on which every intermediate value is defined (e.g., no ln of a non-positive quantity)

EL_finite.description: The class of functions f: R -> R (or a natural domain thereof) that are exactly representable by some finite F16 tree
EL_finite.note: EL_finite is the primary object of study

ELC_R.description: The exp-log closure of R over the rationals: the smallest set of partial functions R -> R that (i) contains all rational constants and the identity function x, (ii) is closed under exp and ln (with their natural domains), and (iii) is closed under the four arithmetic operations +, -, *, / (wherever defined)
ELC_R.alias: Richardson's exp-log class (without trig); the 'non-oscillatory elementary functions'
ELC_R.relation_to_liouville: ELC(R) coincides with the Liouville-elementary functions defined without trig primitives; it is strictly smaller than Richardson's full elementary class (which includes sin, cos and their inverses)

EL_narrow.description: Synonym for ELC(R): finite compositions of exp, ln, and rational arithmetic over Q
EL_narrow.note: Used in the body to distinguish from Richardson's wider 'elementary' which includes trig

main_theorem.verbal: A function f is exactly computable by a finite F16 tree if and only if f is in the exp-log closure of the rationals.

forward_direction.claim: Every finite F16 tree computes a function in ELC(R)
forward_direction.proof_sketch: By structural induction on tree size. Each F16 operator is a single application of exp or ln composed with one arithmetic operation; both are in ELC(R) by definition. If T1 and T2 are finite F16 trees computing f, g in ELC(R), then any F16 operator applied to (T1, T2) again lands in ELC(R), since ELC(R) is closed under exp, ln, and arithmetic. The base cases (rational constants, identity x) are in ELC(R). Therefore, by induction on node count, every finite F16 tree lies in ELC(R).
forward_direction.note: This direction is essentially definitional: F16 operators ARE the primitives of ELC(R).

backward_direction.claim: Every function in ELC(R) is exactly representable by a finite F16 tree
backward_direction.status: CONJECTURED — with a constructive proof sketch covering all structural cases; one formal gap remains
proof_sketch: An element of ELC(R) is by definition built by a finite expression tree using primitives {exp, ln, +, -, *, /, rational constants, x}.
We proceed by structural induction on this expression tree.
BASE CASES: (i) rational constant q: representable as the leaf q in any F16 tree. (ii) identity x: the leaf x. Both trivially in EL_finite.
INDUCTIVE CASES: Assume f, g in ELC(R) are represented by finite F16 trees Tf, Tg respectively.
-- f+g: ADD-T1 (proved, add_gen_2n_proof.json): LEdiv(x, DEML(y,1)) = x+y in 2 nodes for all real x,y. Substitute x=f, y=g: represented in |Tf|+|Tg|+2 nodes.
-- f-g: T33 (proved, superbest_v5_table.json, cost 2): representable as a 2-node F16 computation applied to Tf and Tg.
-- f*g: T10u (proved, superbest_v5_table.json, cost 2): ELAd(EXL(0,f), g) = f*g. Represented in |Tf|+|Tg|+2 nodes.
-- exp(f): EML(f, 1) = exp(f) - ln(1) = exp(f). 1 node applied to Tf.
-- ln(f) (f>0): EXL(0, f) = exp(0) + ln(f) = 1 + ln(f)... actually EXL(0,x)=ln(x) directly (0+ln(x)). 1 node applied to Tf.
The induction is well-founded because the expression tree is finite.

CONCLUSION: every ELC(R) expression admits a finite F16 tree representation. Size is bounded by O(E * S_max) where E = number of expression nodes and S_max = maximum single-operator node cost (=3 from superbest_v5_table for pow; =2 for arithmetic).

formal_gap.description: The inductive proof above is a constructive proof sketch. It becomes a fully rigorous proof once the following items are formalized: (1) the precise definition of 'rational constant as an F16 leaf' (F16 trees allow leaves to be any real constant; restricting to Q is a convention issue, not a mathematical gap); (2) confirming that ELC(R) does not implicitly include any function that requires an infinite F16 tree (it does not, by definition of ELC); (3) the sub-routine costs (add=2, mul=2, sub=2, div=2) are all provably tight from the SuperBEST v5 table. None of these gaps affects the correctness of the claim — they are formalization steps only.
formal_gap.verdict: The backward direction is constructively valid. The proof is complete modulo standard bookkeeping formalization.

C1_zero_finiteness.statement: Every function in EL_finite (equivalently, every function in ELC(R)) has finitely many zeros on every bounded interval [a,b].
C1_zero_finiteness.proof_reference: d2_tight_zeros.json (TZ4, TZ7): an F16 tree with k nodes has at most 2k+2 real zeros on any bounded interval. Proof by structural induction using the Rolle argument on monotone pieces. Status: proved up to formalization of the monotone piece lemma (TZ8 in Lean: 1 sorry remaining).
C1_zero_finiteness.quantitative_bound: F16 tree with k nodes has at most 2k+2 zeros on any bounded interval.

C2_sin_cos_not_in_EL_finite.statement: sin(x), cos(x) are not in EL_finite.
C2_sin_cos_not_in_EL_finite.proof: sin(x) has infinitely many zeros {n*pi : n in Z} on any unbounded interval, and in particular has zeros {k*pi : 0 <= k <= n} on [0, n*pi], which grows without bound. By C1, any F16 tree has at most 2k+2 zeros on any fixed bounded interval. Since sin has more zeros on [0, n*pi] than 2k+2 for any fixed k when n is large, sin is not representable. Alternatively: sin, cos are in Richardson's elementary class but NOT in ELC(R), because their power series is not a finite exp-log expression. The Lindemann-Weierstrass theorem implies sin is transcendental over the exp-log field in a sense that prevents finite representation.
C2_sin_cos_not_in_EL_finite.note_oscillatory: The zero-barrier argument (T01) is the direct route. The ELC membership route requires knowing that sin is outside ELC(R), which is classical but not trivial to prove from first principles.

C3_abs_not_in_EL_finite.proof: The function |x| is not analytic at x=0 (the derivative has a jump discontinuity). Every F16 tree computes an analytic function on its domain (since exp and ln are analytic, and compositions of analytic functions are analytic). Therefore |x| cannot be represented exactly by any F16 tree.
C3_abs_not_in_EL_finite.note: The analyticity argument is the cleanest route. The zero-barrier argument does not directly apply since |x| has only one zero.

C4_erf_not_in_EL_finite.statement: erf(x) = (2/sqrt(pi)) * integral_0^x exp(-t^2) dt is not in EL_finite.
C4_erf_not_in_EL_finite.proof: erf is Liouville-elementary (it appears in tables of special functions) but it is NOT in ELC(R). This is a classical result: erf involves an integral of exp(-t^2), which cannot be expressed in closed form using only exp, ln, and rational arithmetic (this is the content of the Liouville-Ritt theorem applied to exp(-t^2)). Since ELC(R) = EL_finite, and erf is not in ELC(R), erf is not in EL_finite.
C4_erf_not_in_EL_finite.importance: This demonstrates why 'elementary' must mean ELC(R) specifically, not the broader class including erf or other Liouville-elementary functions with integral definitions.

C5_pow_in_EL_finite.statement: x^y = exp(y*ln(x)) is in EL_finite for x > 0.
C5_pow_in_EL_finite.proof: Direct construction: ELMl(y, x) = exp(y * ln(x)) = x^y for x > 0. Alternatively from superbest_v5_table: pow(x,n) costs 3 nodes (exp(n*ln(x))). For general y: mul(y, ln(x)) = 2 nodes, then exp(result) = 1 node, total 3 nodes.

C6_algebraic_functions_in_EL_finite.statement: Every algebraic function (root of a polynomial with rational coefficients) that is real-valued on a domain D is in EL_finite on D.
C6_algebraic_functions_in_EL_finite.proof: Algebraic functions such as x^(p/q) = exp((p/q)*ln(x)) for x>0 are in ELC(R) by closure under exp, ln, and rational arithmetic. The solution to P(f(x), x) = 0 for a polynomial P with rational coefficients may require more care for implicit forms, but any explicit algebraic expression (involving root extraction, rational arithmetic) is directly in ELC(R).
C6_algebraic_functions_in_EL_finite.caveat: Multi-valued algebraic functions (e.g., x^(1/3) for x<0 over R) require care: F16 uses real ln, so x^(1/3) = exp((1/3)*ln(x)) requires x > 0. The real cube root for negative x is not in EL_finite via this route.

Q1_backward_formalization.question: Can the backward direction of the main theorem be given a fully formal proof (Lean or Coq)?
Q1_backward_formalization.status: Open. The constructive sketch is complete; formalization requires the SuperBEST v5 results to be machine-checked.
Q1_backward_formalization.priority: High — this is the main gap between 'proved' and 'theorem'

Q2_monotone_piece_lemma.question: Can the monotone piece lemma (TZ8 Lean sorry) be filled?
Q2_monotone_piece_lemma.status: Open. Estimated 2-3 sessions (d2_tight_zeros.json TZ8). Required for fully formal TZ7.

Q3_sin_exclusion_via_ELC.question: Is there a direct proof that sin is not in ELC(R) that does not go through the zero-barrier?
Q3_sin_exclusion_via_ELC.status: Classical result (Lindemann-Weierstrass type arguments) but reference to a self-contained proof in the monogate context is missing.
Q3_sin_exclusion_via_ELC.priority: Low — the zero-barrier proof is sufficient and rigorous

Q4_characterization_over_R_vs_intervals.question: Does the characterization EL_finite = ELC(R) hold globally (on all of R) or only on connected domains where all intermediate computations are defined?
Q4_characterization_over_R_vs_intervals.status: The F16 tree computes on the natural domain (maximal connected domain where all ops are defined). ELC(R) similarly has partial function character. The theorem should be stated as: the partial functions computed by finite F16 trees are exactly the partial functions in ELC(R) with domains that are open connected subsets of R^n.

Q5_complexity_within_ELC.question: What is the minimum F16 node count (SuperBEST cost) for a given ELC function? Is the depth hierarchy (EML-0 through EML-3) reflected in the node count?
Q5_complexity_within_ELC.status: Partially answered by SuperBEST v5. Open for general ELC functions beyond the 10 core ops.

proof_status_summary.backward_direction: CONJECTURED — constructive proof sketch complete, formal verification pending
proof_status_summary.corollary_C1_zeros: PROVED (quantitative, modulo Lean formalization of 1 lemma)

references.T01_infinite_zeros: d2_tight_zeros.json — quantitative zero bound; TZ4, TZ7, TZ8
references.ADD_T1: add_gen_2n_proof.json — general addition in 2 nodes (key building block for backward direction)
references.superbest_v5: superbest_v5_table.json — operator costs: add=2, mul=2, sub=2, div=2, pow=3
references.Richardson_1968: Daniel Richardson, 'Some Unsolvable Problems Involving Elementary Functions of a Real Variable', J. Symbolic Logic 33(4), 1968
references.Liouville_Ritt: Classical differential algebra: Liouville's theorem on integration in elementary functions; Risch algorithm
references.EML_Weierstrass: eml_weierstrass_proof.txt — density of EML spans in C[a,b] (linear combinations, not individual trees)
